critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed experiment with several notable strengths: equivalent tuning effort for both models, preprocessing fit on training data only, macro-averaged metrics across repositories, ablation, and explicit scoping. I'll focus on genuine methodological vulnerabilities rather than edge-case nitpicks.

**Issue 1: Temporal leakage from random stratified split**

Claim: A repository-stratified 60/20/20 random split with label-stratification produces a valid evaluation that avoids data leakage.

Why it might be wrong: The split is not time-ordered. PRs from October 2024 may appear in training, while PRs from January 2023 appear in test. This creates two interrelated problems. First, author historical defect rate is computed 'on training data only' — but if training contains future PRs from the same author, that author feature encodes future information relative to the test PR's actual merge time. Second, the transformer's pre-trained representations will pick up entity-level signals (file names, author aliases, team identifiers embedded in PR text) that leak organizational patterns from future periods into past-period test examples. The core issue is that defect-introducing behavior is temporally autocorrelated within authors and within code regions. A model trained on any 60% random sample will see future author behavior that it can condition on when predicting past test PRs. This is not a subtle effect — in software quality prediction literature, random splits routinely inflate AUC by 5–15 percentage points relative to time-ordered splits.

Evidence for/against: Compare Brier scores under this stratified random split versus a strict time-cutoff split (train on first 70% of timeline per repository, test on last 15%). A large gap would confirm leakage. Also check whether per-author defect rates in training closely mirror those in test; they should be nearly identical under random split regardless of true concept drift.

**Issue 2: Label construction conflates defect introduction with defect discovery timing**

Claim: A 7–14 day post-merge window captures whether a PR introduced a defect with sufficient causal clarity.

Why it might be wrong: The window measures defect *discovery* speed, not defect introduction. Discovery timing is strongly confounded by PR visibility: high-churn areas of the codebase are exercised sooner post-merge, so defects in low-traffic code modules are systematically missed and labeled negative. This means the positive class is not 'PR introduced a defect' but 'PR introduced a defect in code that gets exercised quickly.' The model then learns to predict code-path heat rather than defect-introduction risk. Furthermore, both the bug-fix commit reference heuristic and the issue tracker link heuristic have asymmetric recall: teams with strong commit-message discipline produce more positives than equally defect-prone teams with lax discipline. The 85% agreement target on ~500 manually reviewed PRs does not resolve this because reviewers are assessing label accuracy against the automated rule, not against independent ground truth of whether the PR actually introduced a latent defect.

Evidence for/against: Stratify test-set Brier score by code module activity (commit frequency in the module over the prior 30 days). If the model's advantage concentrates in high-activity modules and near-zero in low-activity modules, label bias is the driver. Also compute per-repository positive rate and correlate with commit message formalism (fraction of commits following conventional commit format) — a strong correlation suggests the label is measuring team discipline, not defect density.

**Issue 3: The transformer's advantage may come entirely from author identity memorization, not code understanding**

Claim: The transformer outperforms the baseline because it encodes richer semantic signals from code changes and PR text.

Why it might be wrong: The transformer receives PR title, description, and review comments — text that frequently contains author names, team identifiers, and project-specific jargon. With 24–36 months of data per repository, the model can memorize author-level defect rates from the text stream without learning any code semantics, and this memorization will score well on a random split (Issue 1 amplifies this). The ablation described (transformer without text features) does quantify text contribution, but it does not distinguish 'text contributes because of linguistic understanding' from 'text contributes because author names appear in PR titles and descriptions.' If the transformer's gain over the baseline vanishes when author-identifying tokens are masked, the hypothesis — that transformer-based encoding of repository signals provides semantic value — is not supported, even if the Brier score improvement is real.

Evidence for/against: Run a second ablation: transformer with text features but with author names, reviewer names, team tags, and repository-specific identifiers replaced by anonymous tokens. If Brier score drops substantially (to near or below baseline), the gain is identity memorization, not semantics. This is a cheap experiment that directly tests the hypothesis mechanism.

**Issue 4: Macro-averaged Brier score across 10–20 repositories with highly variable base rates is not a stable comparative metric**

Claim: Macro-averaging Brier score across repositories prevents large repositories from dominating and allows fair comparison across teams with different base rates.

Why it might be wrong: Brier score without baseline-rate adjustment is not comparable across repositories with different positive rates. A repository with 5% defect rate has a trivially low Brier score even for a constant-zero predictor (Brier ≈ 0.05 × 0.95 ≈ 0.0475), while a repository with 30% defect rate has a null-model Brier of 0.21. Macro-averaging these raw values means the aggregate is dominated by the easiest repositories (lowest base rate) rather than where the model is doing meaningful work. A transformer that is excellent at easy repositories and mediocre everywhere else will show better macro-averaged Brier than a baseline that is uniformly good but not excellent anywhere, even if the baseline is practically superior for teams that matter.

The design mentions calibration curves and per-repository Brier scores as secondary outputs, but the primary selection metric is the unadjusted macro-average, which means model selection itself is influenced by this bias.

Evidence for/against: Compute the Brier Skill Score (BSS = 1 - Brier_model / Brier_climatology) per repository, where climatology is the constant-prevalence predictor. Macro-average the BSS. If the ranking of transformer vs. baseline changes under BSS versus raw Brier, the unadjusted metric was misleading. This is a straightforward reanalysis of existing results.

**Summary**

Issues 1 and 3 share a root cause: the evaluation does not cleanly isolate temporal or identity-based memorization from genuine semantic generalization. Issue 2 means the label may be measuring something systematically different from what the hypothesis targets. Issue 4 means the primary metric is not invariant to base rate variation across repositories, corrupting both model selection and the final comparison. All four are resolvable through changes to the experimental design (time-ordered split, identity masking ablation, BSS normalization, label validation against code-path activity), not post-hoc rationalization.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split allows future author and code behavior to leak into training when evaluating past-period test PRs, inflating Brier score relative to a time-ordered evaluation.

The 7–14 day post-merge label window measures defect discovery speed, not defect introduction, systematically under-labeling defects in low-traffic code paths and conflating team commit-message discipline with defect density.

The transformer's text-feature advantage may derive from author and team identity memorization in PR titles and comments rather than semantic code understanding, which the planned ablation does not distinguish.

Macro-averaging raw Brier scores across repositories with widely varying defect base rates is base-rate-sensitive and can favor a model that excels on easy (low base rate) repositories over one that is uniformly better on harder repositories.

The repository-stratified random split allows author historical defect rate and temporal text patterns to include future data relative to test PRs, creating systematic leakage that a chronological split would prevent.

The 7–14 day label construction window systematically censors latent defects in complex, high-impact PRs, potentially inverting the correlation between complexity features and observed labels for the most dangerous PRs.

Macro-averaging raw Brier scores across repositories with heterogeneous base rates conflates task difficulty with model quality, and a metric like Brier Skill Score normalized by climatology should be the primary comparison instead.

Repository-stratified random split allows future observations to appear in training, violating temporal causality and potentially inflating both models' scores in ways that may not be symmetric between transformer and baseline.

Author historical defect rate, despite being computed on training data only, is temporally contaminated because training data is drawn from a random 60% of a 36-month window rather than a strict temporal prefix.

The 7–14 day defect labeling window creates truncated-observation false negatives that are correlated with file category and PR type, systematically mislabeling slow-surfacing defects as negatives in ways that distort the learned signal rather than adding symmetric noise.

issues_raised: The repository-stratified random split allows author historical defect rate and temporal text patterns to include future data relative to test PRs, creating systematic leakage that a chronological split would prevent.

The 7–14 day label construction window systematically censors latent defects in complex, high-impact PRs, potentially inverting the correlation between complexity features and observed labels for the most dangerous PRs.

Macro-averaging raw Brier scores across repositories with heterogeneous base rates conflates task difficulty with model quality, and a metric like Brier Skill Score normalized by climatology should be the primary comparison instead.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment with several good design choices (stratified bootstrap CIs, ablations, separate calibration curves, validation-only tuning). But there are three genuine methodological issues worth pressing on.

---

**Issue 1: Temporal leakage via the stratified random split**

The methodology deliberately uses a repository-stratified *random* split rather than a chronological split. The stated justification is avoiding fold domination by short-lived process changes. But this creates a specific and serious leakage mechanism: features derived from historical context (most notably, *author historical defect rate*) are computed using training data. If that training data includes PRs that temporally follow the test PR — which is likely under a random split — then the author defect rate feature encodes future information at prediction time.

More subtly, even the transformer model will see PR text and metadata from time windows that postdate test PRs during training. In production the model only ever sees history; in the random split evaluation it implicitly has access to future patterns (e.g., how a particular author's coding patterns or PR language evolved). The stated mitigation — that merge timestamp is retained as a feature — does not address this; retaining timestamp as a feature is not the same as enforcing temporal ordering of the split.

This is not a theoretical worry. Author defect rate is explicitly listed as a feature, and it is described as "computed on training data only." Under a random split, training data for test-set PRs authored in month 6 of the observation window will include months 7–36. The feature is therefore constructed from a population that includes future data relative to that PR.

What would constitute evidence: Compare model performance under the random split versus a strict chronological split (train on first 60% of each repository's timeline, validate on next 20%, test on final 20%). If Brier score degrades substantially under the temporal split, that is strong evidence of leakage. If performance is comparable, the mechanism matters less in practice — but even then, the deployed model will always be in a forward-prediction regime, so the temporal split is the correct evaluation.

---

**Issue 2: Label construction creates systematic censoring correlated with PR complexity**

The positive label requires that within 7–14 days post-merge, either a bug-fix commit references the PR or a high-severity ticket is filed. This is a censored observation window with a specific failure mode: defects introduced by complex, high-risk PRs that touch shared infrastructure often manifest weeks or months later, not within 7–14 days. Defects in isolated features or small utility changes tend to be found quickly (they are exercised sooner, reviewed more carefully post-hoc, or caught by adjacent tests).

The consequence is a systematic mislabeling pattern: high-complexity PRs that actually introduced latent defects are labeled negative (no defect detected in window), while the model is simultaneously trained on features correlated with complexity (lines changed, files modified, etc.). This means the model learns a distorted signal — complexity features may be *negatively* correlated with observed labels for the most dangerous PRs — and Brier score on test reflects performance on this censored label, not on true defect introduction.

The 85% manual label agreement target partly mitigates this, but only for a 500-PR sample, and it cannot detect the systematic censoring bias if reviewers also have no visibility into defects that manifest after 14 days.

What would constitute evidence: Construct a held-out cohort of PRs for which 90+ day follow-up is available. Compare the 7–14 day label against the 90-day label for this cohort. If large, high-complexity PRs show substantially higher label revision rates (initially labeled negative, later labeled positive), the censoring mechanism is active and the evaluation's Brier score is inflated for the specific PRs the system most needs to catch. A regression of label revision rate on PR size features would quantify this.

---

**Issue 3: Macro-averaging Brier score across repositories with heterogeneous base rates miscalibrates the comparison**

The primary metric is Brier score macro-averaged across repositories. Brier score is explicitly baseline-rate-dependent: a classifier that always predicts the base rate achieves Brier score p(1-p), which varies from ~0.10 (10% base rate) to ~0.25 (50% base rate). Repositories with higher defect prevalence will mechanically generate larger Brier score contributions under macro-averaging, not because the model is worse there, but because the task is intrinsically harder.

More critically, the transformer and baseline may perform differently across this heterogeneity in a way that macro-averaging obscures. If the transformer is better on high-prevalence repositories (which dominate the raw Brier score) but worse on low-prevalence ones, macro-averaging will mask this. If the baseline is better calibrated on low-prevalence repositories, that clinical insight is lost.

The proposed mitigation — per-repository Brier score as a secondary metric — helps for detecting outliers, but the *primary* comparison metric is still the macro-average, which is the number that will drive the "transformer wins / loses" conclusion.

What would constitute evidence: Report the Brier Skill Score (BSS = 1 - Brier / Brier_climatology) per repository, then macro-average skill scores rather than raw Brier scores. This normalizes out base-rate heterogeneity. If the rank-ordering of models changes between macro-Brier and macro-BSS, the raw macro-average was misleading. Additionally, check whether the transformer's claimed advantage concentrates in high-base-rate repositories — if so, it may be an artifact of those repositories having more signal-rich PRs, not a generalizable text-understanding advantage.

---

**Summary assessment:**

Issue 1 (temporal leakage) is the most structurally serious because it affects the validity of the entire comparison — if both models benefit from the random split but the transformer benefits more (plausible, since the pre-trained LM can exploit subtler temporal text patterns), the claimed advantage is inflated. Issue 2 (label censoring) undermines the claim that the evaluation reflects real defect risk, particularly for the highest-stakes PRs. Issue 3 (macro-Brier miscalibration) is the least severe but could affect the headline conclusion. Together they are sufficient to question whether the results, as specified, would generalize to deployment.

issues_raised: Repository-stratified random split allows future observations to appear in training, violating temporal causality and potentially inflating both models' scores in ways that may not be symmetric between transformer and baseline.

Author historical defect rate, despite being computed on training data only, is temporally contaminated because training data is drawn from a random 60% of a 36-month window rather than a strict temporal prefix.

The 7–14 day defect labeling window creates truncated-observation false negatives that are correlated with file category and PR type, systematically mislabeling slow-surfacing defects as negatives in ways that distort the learned signal rather than adding symmetric noise.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment design with several commendable features: separate tuning and test sets, stratified bootstrapping for CIs, ablations, manual label validation, and equivalent tuning budget for both models. That said, there are two genuine methodological flaws, grouped by their shared root cause.

---

**Root Cause 1: Temporal leakage through the split design**

**Issue 1: Repository-stratified random split violates temporal causality**

The methodology uses a repository-stratified split that preserves class balance and repository mix — but does so by randomly sampling across the full 24–36 month observation horizon. This means training examples can be drawn from, say, month 30, while test examples come from month 12 of the same repository. Any feature that encodes time-correlated patterns (code style evolution, team composition drift, changing defect rates as a team grows or shrinks, technology migrations) will be learned from the 'future' and evaluated on the 'past.' In practice this gives the model access to information it would not have in deployment.

The specific mechanism: author historical defect rate, CI pass rate, and repository-level trends are computed across the full window but partitioned randomly. An author's defect rate computed on the training set may incorporate their activity from months that temporally follow the test PR being predicted. Even if the scalar feature is computed on training data only, the feature design itself is calibrated to a shuffled timeline.

What would constitute evidence: Compare Brier scores from the current random-stratified split against a strict temporal holdout (last N months per repository held out). If the random split inflates both models' scores roughly equally, the comparison is still valid. If the transformer gains disproportionately more from the random split (likely if it extracts time-correlated linguistic patterns), then the hypothesis test is contaminated. The ablation removing text features partially addresses this but doesn't resolve the author-feature leakage.

**Issue 2: Author historical defect rate feature is computed on training data only — but the split is random, so 'training data' includes future observations for some authors**

This is a specific instantiation of Issue 1. The design says author defect rate is 'computed on training data only,' which sounds correct. But if training data is a random 60% draw from 36 months, then for a given test PR from month 8, the author's defect rate is computed from a training set that may include their month 25 behavior. This is not a minor noise issue — author defect rate is likely a high-signal feature and its leakage would systematically inflate calibration quality.

What would constitute evidence: Compute author defect rate with a strict temporal cutoff (only PRs prior to the test PR's timestamp). Rerun and compare Brier scores. A meaningful drop (>0.01 Brier) would confirm the leakage is material.

---

**Root Cause 2: Labeling window creates asymmetric noise that favors structured features**

**Issue 3: The 7–14 day defect label window is a truncated-observation problem, not a clean label**

The label construction anchors on whether a bug-fix commit references the PR within 7–14 days, or a high-severity ticket is filed in that window. This creates a systematic false-negative problem: defects introduced by a PR but discovered outside the window are silently labeled negative. The noise rate is not uniform — it is correlated with PR type, codebase area, and team responsiveness. PRs touching infrastructure, security, or batch-processed data pipelines tend to produce defects that surface weeks or months later. PRs touching UI or user-facing APIs tend to surface quickly.

The mechanism of failure: the baseline's hand-engineered features include file categories, which will correlate with the truncation bias. The transformer, processing code paths and file names, may learn similar correlations. Both models will learn 'PRs touching fast-feedback areas appear more defect-prone' because those defects are more likely to fall within the labeling window — not because those PRs are actually more dangerous. This is not a labeling error in the conventional sense; it is a structural mismatch between the label definition and the underlying phenomenon. The 85% manual agreement target only validates that the automated rule is applied consistently, not that the 7–14 day window captures the right defects.

What would constitute evidence: Stratify post-hoc by file category and compare label rates vs. known industry defect latency distributions for those categories. If infrastructure/batch PRs are systematically under-labeled relative to UI PRs, the bias is present. Alternatively, extend the labeling window to 30 or 60 days on a subset and measure how label flip rates differ by file type.

---

**Summary**

The most consequential flaw is Issue 1/2 (temporal leakage via random stratified split). This affects the validity of the comparison because the two models may benefit from the leakage differently, and in particular it undermines the author defect rate feature which is likely high signal. Issue 3 (truncated observation labeling) affects both models equally so it weakens external validity of the absolute Brier scores without necessarily invalidating the A/B comparison — but it does mean the reported results reflect a specific truncated-observation task, not 'post-merge defect prediction' in the general sense.

The experiment is recoverable: switching to a temporal holdout (last N months per repository) and recomputing author features with temporal cutoffs would address Issues 1 and 2. The labeling window issue is harder to fix without rearchitecting data collection but can be characterized with sensitivity analysis.
